title: ST 558 HW 4 - Ben’s version
text:
Basic EDA
Lets check the summary of the data to make sure everything looks okay

summary(rcs)

      Coaster           Park            City           State    
 Length   :635   Length   :635   Length   :635   Length   :635  
 N.unique :482   N.unique :197   N.unique :171   N.unique : 40  
 N.blank  :  0   N.blank  :  0   N.blank  :  0   N.blank  :  0  
 Min.nchar:  2   Min.nchar:  6   Min.nchar:  3   Min.nchar:  4  
 Max.nchar: 46   Max.nchar: 47   Max.nchar: 20   Max.nchar: 14

 Midwest  :134   Min.   :1920   1:Older : 69   Min.   :  6.00   Min.   :  5.0

                 3rd Qu.:2013                  3rd Qu.: 55.00   3rd Qu.:109.0

    Duration   Inversions Inversions.Count        Make    
 Min.   : 20   No :465    Min.   :0.0000   Length   :635  
 1st Qu.: 70   Yes:170    1st Qu.:0.0000   N.unique : 54  
 Median :101              Median :0.0000   N.blank  : 36

Seems like everything checks out. Lets check the counts of missing values in the data by column

colSums(is.na(rcs))

         Coaster             Park             City            State 
               0                0                0                0 
   Census.Region      Year.Opened         AgeGroup         TopSpeed 
               0                0                0               74 
       MaxHeight            Scale             Type           Design 
               8                0                0                0 
            Drop           Length         Duration       Inversions 
             128               33               66                0 
Inversions.Count             Make 
               0                0 


Drop, TopSeed, and Duration are the variables with the most missing values. They’re missing 128, 74, and 66 values respectively.

title: ST 558 HW 4 - Ben’s version
text:
Categorical Variables
Lets create some contingency tables using table()

#one-way table
table(rcs$Scale, useNA = 'always')


Extreme  Family  Kiddie  Thrill    <NA> 
    269     178      28     160       0 


From the table we can see there are 269 extreme roller coasters in the dataset

#two-way table
table(rcs$Scale, rcs$Census.Region, useNA = 'always')

         
          Midwest Northeast South West <NA>
  Extreme      61        62    92   54    0
  Family       32        49    68   29    0
  Kiddie        8         7     6    7    0
  Thrill       33        38    57   32    0
  <NA>          0         0     0    0    0


From the table we can see there are 49 extreme roller coasters in the Midwest

#three-way table
table(rcs$Scale, rcs$Census.Region, rcs$AgeGroup, useNA = 'always')

, ,  = 1:Older

         
          Midwest Northeast South West <NA>
  Extreme       6         3     5    3    0
  Family        2         7     4    0    0
  Kiddie        1         3     0    1    0
  Thrill        8         8     8   10    0
  <NA>          0         0     0    0    0

, ,  = 2:Recent

         
          Midwest Northeast South West <NA>
  Extreme      21        22    38   24    0
  Family        9        13     9    9    0
  Kiddie        3         3     0    3    0
  Thrill       12        10    10    4    0
  <NA>          0         0     0    0    0

, ,  = 3:Newest

         
          Midwest Northeast South West <NA>
  Extreme      34        37    49   27    0
  Family       21        29    55   20    0
  Kiddie        4         1     6    3    0
  Thrill       13        20    39   18    0
  <NA>          0         0     0    0    0

, ,  = NA

         
          Midwest Northeast South West <NA>
  Extreme       0         0     0    0    0
  Family        0         0     0    0    0
  Kiddie        0         0     0    0    0
  Thrill        0         0     0    0    0
  <NA>          0         0     0    0    0


From the table we can see there are 7 extreme roller coasters in the Midwest that are considered to be ‘older’
